question: Mercury's perihelion precession: how much does GR contribute?

options: Total observed precession ~5600''/century; planetary perturbations explain $\sim 5557''$; residual 43''/century explained exactly by GR through metric correction $\sim r_s/r$ at perihelion. First successful application of GR (Einstein 1915) before formal completion of theory
GR contributes only 1''/century — negligible (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses) (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements)
All precession is Newtonian; GR plays no role (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses) (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum)
GR predicts precession opposite to observed direction (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder)
passive.explanation: Mercury orbital data: $a = 0.387$ AU, $e = 0.206$, $P = 88$ d. Observed perihelion advance: 5600''/century. Newtonian: precession from oblate Sun, Jupiter/Venus etc.\ accounts for 5557''/century. Residual 43''/century plagued astronomers for 70 years (Le Verrier postulated hypothetical planet Vulcan inside Mercury's orbit). Einstein computed exactly $\delta\phi = 6\pi GM/(c^2 a(1-e^2))$ per orbit $= 42.98''/$century — match to 1\% from theory. Now sub-percent. Other planets: smaller effects. Binary pulsars: huge precession ($\sim 4°$/year for Hulse-Taylor, $16°$/year for double pulsar). Lab-scale: gravitomagnetic precession measured in atomic interferometry.

active.prompt: Match each classical test of GR with its essence:

pairs.definition: 43''/century anomaly; first GR triumph (1915); confirmed metric correction at $r_s/r$
1.75'' at solar limb; Eddington 1919; modern VLBI $\gamma = 1$ to $10^{-5}$
Extra light travel time near Sun; Cassini 2002: $\gamma_{\rm PPN}$ to $2\times 10^{-5}$

active.explanation: Four classical tests of GR (Mercury perihelion + light bending + redshift + Shapiro delay) all confirmed; 5th 'modern' test = GW emission from binary pulsars (Hulse-Taylor, Nobel 1993). 6th = direct GW detection (LIGO 2015, Nobel 2017). 7th = BH imaging (EHT M87* 2019, Sgr A* 2022).

question: Gravity Probe B: how did it test GR's frame-dragging prediction?

options: Polar-orbit satellite (2004-2010) with 4 superconducting gyroscopes; measured (i) geodetic precession (6606 mas/yr predicted, observed 6602 ± 18) due to motion through curved space; (ii) frame-dragging (Lense-Thirring) 37 mas/yr predicted, observed 37 ± 7 — both confirm GR. Precision $\sim 0.3\%$ on geodetic, $\sim 20\%$ on frame-dragging
Direct measurement of speed of gravity in laboratory (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements) (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses)
Test of equivalence principle only (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD) (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder)

GP-B contradicted GR by 50\% (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses) (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables)
passive.explanation: Gravity Probe B (NASA, $\sim$\$760M, 47 years from concept to result): 4 cryogenic quartz-rotor gyros in Stanford-designed satellite. Two GR predictions: (1) geodetic effect from gyro moving through curved space, axis tilts toward orbital plane direction — analogous to Thomas precession but gravitational, $\delta\phi_{\rm geo} = (3GM/2c^2 r)\Omega_{\rm orbit}$; (2) frame-dragging from Earth's rotation pulls inertial frames around, $\delta\phi_{LT} = GJ_\oplus/c^2 r^3$ direction. Geodetic: observed $-6601.8 \pm 18.3$ mas/yr vs.\ predicted $-6606.1$ — agreement at $0.3\%$. Frame-dragging: observed $-37.2 \pm 7.2$ mas/yr vs.\ $-39.2$ — agreement at $\sim 19\%$ (data-analysis challenges with electrostatic patch effects). Complementary: LARES satellites also test Lense-Thirring.

question: Event Horizon Telescope: what did it directly image and what does it test?

options: Global VLBI array (8+ stations, $\lambda = 1.3$ mm, baseline $\sim$ Earth diameter) imaged 'shadow' of M87* (2019) and Sgr A* (2022) supermassive BHs; shadow diameter $\approx 5.2\,r_s$ (Schwarzschild) consistent with Kerr metric; tests no-hair theorem at horizon scales; rules out exotic horizon-less objects ('boson stars' etc.) for these objects
Image of a single solar-mass black hole at sub-parsec distance (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements)

Detection of Hawking radiation from M87* (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables) (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)
Direct measurement of Hawking temperature (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses)
passive.explanation: EHT: world's largest virtual telescope via VLBI synthesis. Sgr A* (Milky Way centre, $4.3\times 10^6\,M_\odot$, 8 kpc): angular size $\sim 50\,\mu$as. M87* ($6.5\times 10^9\,M_\odot$, 16 Mpc): $\sim 40\,\mu$as. EHT achieves $\sim 20\,\mu$as resolution. 'Shadow' = central dark region surrounded by photon ring; ring diameter $\sim \sqrt{27}\,r_s$ for Schwarzschild ($5.2 r_s$) — relatively insensitive to spin. M87* (Apr 2019): asymmetric ring with brightness modulation from Doppler beaming. Sgr A* (May 2022): similar pattern but variable on minute timescales. Tests: shadow size/shape consistent with Kerr; constrains 'horizon-less' alternatives (gravastars, boson stars, fuzzballs) and modified gravity. Future: next-gen EHT to image dynamics, jet launching, polarisation maps.

question: Double pulsar PSR J0737-3039A/B: what makes it the best lab for GR?

options: Two pulsars in 2.45-hour orbit, both visible; allows 7 post-Keplerian parameters: $\dot\omega, \dot P_b, \gamma, r$ (Shapiro range), $s$ (Shapiro shape), $\Omega_{SO}$ (spin-orbit precession), mass ratio. All 7 consistent with GR within 0.05\% — strongest collective test of GR in strong-field regime
Single pulsar; not a useful binary (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements)
Only orbital period decay measurable; no other tests (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)
Pulsars used here for navigation, not GR tests (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)
passive.explanation: PSR J0737-3039 (Burgay et al.\ 2003): unique two-visible-pulsar system. A: 22.7 ms; B: 2.77 s. Orbit: 2.45 h, $e = 0.088$, almost edge-on (inclination $\sim 88°$ — strong Shapiro signal). Mass-mass diagram: 7 post-Keplerian parameters all give curves in $(M_A, M_B)$ plane; for GR true, all curves must cross at single point — tested to $\lesssim 0.05\%$ (Kramer et al. 2021). Includes: $\dot\omega = 16.9°$/yr (perihelion precession), $\dot P_b = -1.25\times 10^{-12}$ (GW emission), $\gamma = 0.385$ ms (Einstein time dilation), $r, s$ (Shapiro), $\Omega_{SO}$ (geodetic precession of pulsar B spin), mass ratio. World's most precise strong-field GR test. Future: SKA pulsar timing should improve by orders of magnitude.

question: Constraints on modified gravity from GW170817: what did the multi-messenger observation rule out?

options: Coincident GW + GRB with delay $\le 1.7$ s over 40 Mpc constrains $|c_{GW} - c|/c \lesssim 10^{-15}$; kills wide classes of modified gravity (Horndeski with non-luminal GWs, dark energy from scalar-tensor with $c_T \neq c$, Hořava-Lifshitz, generic massive gravity); reduces viable dark-energy/modified-gravity space dramatically

Confirmed extra dimensions (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements) (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)
Showed $c_{GW} = 0.5c$ — large modification (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses)
Ruled out general relativity (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)
passive.explanation: GW170817 + GRB 170817A: GW signal arrives $\sim 1.7$ s before $\gamma$-ray burst over 40 Mpc propagation. Constraint $|c_T - c|/c < 7\times 10^{-16}$ (taking conservative end of GRB delay being intrinsic). This excludes huge swathes of modified gravity. Horndeski theories: most general scalar-tensor with 2nd-order EoM; subclasses $G_4, G_5$ giving $c_T \neq c$ — ruled out. Surviving Horndeski: 'cosmologically viable' subset much smaller. Massive gravity: graviton mass < $10^{-22}$ eV. Extra dimensions: KK gravitons could give different speed — strongly constrained. Lorentz-violating gravity (Hořava): cT vs cmatter speed constraints. Modified gravity with $c_T = c$ (e.g. $f(R)$, traditional Brans-Dicke) survives this test but still subject to other constraints.

question: Tests of GR in the strong-field regime: what new windows have opened in the past decade?

options: (1) LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA GW observations of BBH/BNS mergers: tests inspiral-merger-ringdown waveforms vs Kerr; QNM spectroscopy; (2) EHT BH shadow imaging: tests metric near horizon; (3) GRAVITY VLT interferometry of star S2 around Sgr A*: detected gravitational redshift, Schwarzschild precession; (4) precision pulsar timing in dense stellar regions; (5) X-ray spectroscopy of accretion discs

Only solar system tests have improved (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses)

All strong-field tests have failed; no GR confirmation (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder) (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables)
Lab-scale precision improved but no astrophysical advances (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)
passive.explanation: Strong-field regime ($\Phi/c^2 \sim 0.1$ near BH, vs $10^{-9}$ in solar system) is where modifications to GR most likely show up. Recent advances: (1) GW150914 onward: inspiral-merger-ringdown templates from numerical relativity match observed waveforms to $\lesssim 10\%$ — pre-merger inspiral constrains PN expansion, ringdown tests Kerr QNMs (preliminary BH spectroscopy). (2) EHT M87* (2019), Sgr A* (2022): shadow size/shape consistent with Kerr to $\sim 10\%$. (3) GRAVITY collab (ESO VLT): star S2 around Sgr A* — pericentre passage 2018 detected gravitational redshift, 2020 Schwarzschild precession ($\sim 12'$/orbit); rules out compact dark mass distributions. (4) PSR J0740+6620: most massive NS measured ($2.08 M_\odot$, NICER + Shapiro) — constrains nuclear EoS in extreme conditions. (5) Reflection spectroscopy of accreting BHs: iron K$\alpha$ line shape probes ISCO, spin.
